# Germany, Cyanide and the Habit of Delivering Early

We flew from Jan Smuts in the late winter, Antonette and our three small children and I, with more suitcases than the airline preferred and a folder of AECI papers in my hand luggage that I had been told, in so many words, not to lose. The folder contained the technical specification for a reactor that did not yet exist, written in a mixture of English engineering language and the German metric conventions that I had spent the last fortnight in Sasolburg teaching myself to read without flinching. Somewhere on the other end of the flight, in a yard outside Dusseldorf, a contractor was waiting to build that reactor for us. My job, in the politest possible language, was to make sure he built the one we had paid for and not the one he would have preferred to build.

I had never been to Europe in my life. I had not, until that point, been further from the East Rand than the Cape. The continent on the cabin map looked, from where I sat with a child on each knee and a third asleep against Antonette's shoulder, like a country I had borrowed someone else's nerve to visit.

## Dusseldorf in Winter

Dusseldorf in those years was the kind of industrial city that did not waste hospitality on visiting engineers. It received us politely, found us an apartment in a suburb the contractor's office had pre-approved, gave us a small German car that I never quite stopped over-revving on the autobahns, and got down to business on the Monday morning of the following week. I learned, in the first round of meetings, three things about doing technical business in Germany that have stayed with me through every overseas assignment since.

The first was that the specification was sacred. In South Africa we had treated a specification as the opening position in a negotiation that would unfold, in practice, over the life of the project. In Germany the specification was the project. Any deviation from it, however small, was treated as an event in its own right, with its own paperwork, its own meeting, and its own quietly disapproving silence from the contractor's senior people. I came to appreciate that discipline within a fortnight. It saved an enormous amount of argument later.

The second was that punctuality was not a courtesy. It was an instrument of work. A meeting that began at eight o'clock began at eight o'clock, and the man who arrived at five past eight had, by his arrival, made a statement about himself that the room would not easily forget. I adjusted within a week. I have not, in the years since, adjusted back.

The third was that the contractor's project manager, a man whose name I will not put on the page but whose face I can still see clearly, had decided, before I arrived, that the South African engineer assigned to his site was probably going to be a nuisance. He was a courteous man. He did not say so. But the early meetings had a particular quality of formal politeness that, in any language, means a senior person is waiting to find out whether the junior person across the table is going to be worth speaking to properly.

I was determined that he would decide, by the end of the first month, that I was.

## The Specification Meetings

The first phase of the work, before any steel was cut, was a sequence of specification meetings in a panelled room above the contractor's workshop. There must have been forty of those meetings in the end. They began at eight, they ran until lunch, they resumed at one, and they finished, on the contractor's clock, at five precisely. Coffee was strong, water was cold, and the only thing on the table apart from the documents was a small dish of hard sweets that nobody touched.

What I learned in those meetings was that I had been sent to Germany not because I was the most technically gifted engineer at AECI, which I plainly was not, but because the company needed someone who could sit, for hour after hour, across a table from men who knew the technology better than he did, and refuse to be intimidated into agreeing with things that did not suit the plant we were going to have to run when they had all gone home.

I asked, in those meetings, a great many more questions than the contractor had been expecting. Some of them were technical. Many of them were not. I wanted to know, in plain language, why a particular nozzle was sized the way it was, what the tolerance on a particular weld assumed about the operators who would maintain it, what the cleaning regime was going to look like in a Sasolburg summer when the cooling water temperature was nothing like the German benchmark on which the design assumed. The contractor's people, to their credit, answered. They answered, at first, with a slight air of indulgence, the way a senior engineer answers a graduate. By the end of the second week the indulgence had gone out of the answers, and the answers had become better.

I noticed, somewhere in the middle of all of that, a habit of mind in myself that I had not previously had a name for. I was instinctively pulling each design decision apart, asking what it would mean for the man on the spanner end of the job, and reassembling it on the page in front of me in the form of a question the contractor had to answer. That habit of disassembly and re-assembly, which I now understand to have been a kind of analytical engineering reflex, was the thing the German project manager eventually decided was worth speaking to properly. The formal politeness, in the third week, began quietly to thaw.

## Persuading a Contractor to Run Nights

The project plan, when it was finally signed off, ran to a duration that the AECI head office in Johannesburg had accepted as inevitable, but which I had come to believe was longer than it needed to be. The reactor work was bottlenecked, not by the technology, but by the contractor's working hours. The workshop ran a generous single day shift, with a long lunch in the middle of it, and went quiet from the early evening until the following morning. If the workshop ran nights, even a partial night shift on the critical-path operations, the project would finish weeks earlier than the bar chart suggested.

In South Africa I would have raised it on a Monday morning and the second shift would have started on the Wednesday. In Germany I had to do it differently.

I went, first, to the contractor's senior people, with a small folder of numbers I had prepared in the evenings in our Dusseldorf apartment. I had laid out, in their own format and in their own units, what an earlier completion would mean for the AECI plant in Sasolburg, what it would mean in landed cost for the South African economy, and, more importantly for the conversation in that room, what it would mean for the contractor's standing on the next round of tenders. I made no demand. I made an argument. I left the folder behind and I went away.

It took, in the end, the better part of three weeks of careful conversation. There were meetings in which I said nothing, because the contractor's senior people needed to talk to one another in German without a foreigner in the room, and I had learned by then to make myself useful by being somewhere else. There were meetings in which I produced a single sheet of paper, no more, and answered the questions the senior people had brought to it. There was a Friday afternoon, towards the end of those three weeks, when the project manager looked at me across the long table and said, in the careful English he had been speaking to me for two months, that the workshop would run a partial night shift on the reactor operations from the following Monday, on condition that I would personally be on site at the start of each of those shifts for the first fortnight.

I was on site. I was on site at six in the evening when the night crew came in, I was on site at two in the morning when the senior welders relieved their juniors on the critical seams, and I was on site, more often than I would like to admit, when the day shift came back in at eight. Antonette never reproached me for those weeks. The children, who had begun to make small German friends in the apartment block, accepted that their father had become a man who came home to sleep rather than to live.

The reactor was finished, in the end, almost a full month ahead of the schedule the AECI head office had been expecting.

## Munich, and a Second Yard

Part way through the Dusseldorf work the project picked up a second leg in Munich, on a related reactor for the same downstream chemistry. I shuttled, for several months, between the two yards on the German rail system, with the folder of papers thickening as the project went on, and with a pattern of work that I have used in every project of any scale since.

The pattern was simple to describe and difficult to live. I spent the first day of any week on the older site, walking the work in progress with the foreman and the senior welders and noting, in a small notebook of my own, anything I had nearly missed on the previous visit. I spent the second and third days on the newer site, applying the same discipline of fresh attention. I spent the fourth day with the contractor's planners, in a room with a large board, reconciling what I had seen with what their bar chart claimed. I spent the fifth day writing a single, clear note for the AECI head office in Johannesburg that said, in plain language, what was on track, what was not, and what I was doing about each line on the second list. I posted it on the Friday afternoon. I never missed a Friday.

What I learned, in the shuttle between Dusseldorf and Munich, was that the engineer who travels between two sites carries something the people who never leave either one cannot easily carry. He carries the comparison. He sees the small differences in housekeeping, in toolbox discipline, in the way a foreman runs his Monday morning, that any single yard, looked at on its own, will quietly normalise. I borrowed, in those months, the best small habits of one yard for the other, and the contractor's senior people noticed. Both yards finished better than either of them, on its own, would have done.

## Thirty Days Early

The number that the AECI executives in Johannesburg eventually wrote on a memorandum, when the project was closed out, was that the German work had finished approximately thirty days ahead of the contractual schedule, and that the early completion had unlocked, by the company's own internal calculation, the better part of thirty million rand of value in 1980s money. I will not pretend to have run those numbers myself. The savings were the cumulative effect of an earlier start-up of the South African plant, a forward sale of product into a market that was, at that moment, short of supply, and a series of follow-on contractual effects that the planning people in Johannesburg understood better than I did.

What I did understand, and what I have carried in my bones ever since, was the underlying lesson. A project does not finish early because somebody works harder at the end. It finishes early because somebody, at the beginning, refuses to accept that the schedule on the page is the schedule the project has to keep. The thirty days, when they came, had been quietly assembled out of the night-shift conversation in week six, the specification arguments in weeks one to four, the shuttle pattern between two sites in the middle months, and the Friday notes that had kept the head office close enough to the work to back the decisions when they had to be backed.

I flew home with my family in the early summer, in a different mood from the one in which I had flown out. The folder in my hand luggage was thinner. The continent on the cabin map looked smaller. I had become, somewhere in the German winter, a different kind of engineer.

## Cyanide for the Gold Mines

The promotion that waited for me in Sasolburg was to the management of the cyanide plant. Cyanide, in the South African context, is not an exotic chemical. It is a working tool of the gold-mining industry, used in the leaching processes that recover gold from milled ore, and the gold mines of the Witwatersrand were, in those years, the dominant customers of the AECI cyanide operation. The volumes were enormous, the chain of custody was strict, and the consequences of a supply interruption ran, by the time they had propagated through the mining industry, into the production planning of half the country.

I came into that plant with two convictions and a notebook. The convictions were that the plant was capable of more than it was producing, and that the reason it was not producing more was not a shortage of engineering but a shortage of analytical attention to its own operation. The notebook was the same private notebook I had begun keeping in the VCM years, in which, every Friday, I wrote down the three things I had nearly missed that week.

The first thing I did was walk the plant. I walked it in the way the German shuttle had taught me to walk a yard, which is to say I walked it on a Monday morning with the senior foreman, on a Tuesday afternoon with the analytical chemist on duty, on a Wednesday night with the night-shift charge hand, and on a Thursday with whichever operator had been on the plant the longest. I asked, in each of those walks, what the plant was being prevented from doing, and I listened, often for a great deal longer than was comfortable, to the answers.

The answers, when I had collected enough of them, formed a pattern. The plant was not bottlenecked in the place its instrumentation suggested. It was bottlenecked, in a quieter and more chronic way, in a series of small operational compromises that had accumulated over the years and had come to be regarded as the normal state of affairs. A reactor that was being run a little below its design temperature because an early operator, long since retired, had been nervous of running it any closer to the limit. A feed sequence that had been adjusted to suit a particular crew's preference and never adjusted back. A maintenance window that had been lengthened by half a shift, ten years previously, on the recommendation of a fitter who had since left the company, and which nobody had ever asked to revisit.

None of those compromises, taken on its own, was wrong. Taken together, they were costing the plant something on the order of half its potential throughput.

## Fifty Per Cent More, Without a New Plant

I worked the corrections through, one at a time, over the better part of a year. I did not announce a programme. I did not put a poster on the wall. I sat with the senior operators, one crew at a time, and I asked, with the analytical patience the German months had taught me, whether a particular compromise still needed to be in place. Sometimes the answer was yes, and the compromise stayed. More often the answer, when it was looked at squarely, was that the compromise had outlived its original reason, and could be retired without harm.

By the end of that year the plant was supplying, on a sustainable basis, roughly fifty per cent more cyanide to the gold-mining industry than it had been producing when I had arrived. There was no new capital. There was no new equipment of any significance. There was only a plant that had been allowed, by patient operational adjustment, to do what its original designers had always intended it to do. The mining customers, who had been managing their own supply anxieties for years, were both relieved and, for a while, mildly suspicious. Increases of that order, in an established commodity, were not what they were used to seeing from a long-standing supplier.

The peroxide plant followed, in due course, with a similar story in a different chemistry. I will not repeat the detail. The pattern was the same: a plant that had quietly settled into a fraction of its potential, a series of small operational compromises that nobody had recently questioned, and a steady, careful, crew-by-crew renegotiation of what the plant was capable of when its own people were given permission to stop accepting the compromises as permanent.

## The Analytical Habit, Named

It was somewhere in the middle of those two plant turnarounds that I gave a name, for the first time, to the habit of mind that had been forming in me since the Dusseldorf specification meetings. I had begun to think of it, in my own private language, as analytical engineering. It was not a school of management. It was not a method. It was a discipline, of looking at any operation as a structure of assumptions, and of pulling those assumptions apart one at a time to see which of them still deserved to be there.

I want to set out, as plainly as I can, what that discipline actually consisted of in the years it was being formed. The components were these.

- A refusal to accept any operational compromise as permanent without first understanding what its original reason had been. Half the compromises in any mature plant, I came to believe, are widows of reasons that have long since died.
- A bias to walking the work. The German months had taught me that an engineer who does his thinking in his office is being out-thought, on average, by the engineer who does his thinking on the floor. I never went back to the desk-bound version of the job.
- A weekly written note. The Friday memoranda I had sent from Germany to Johannesburg had taught me that the discipline of compressing a week's observations into a single clear page is, in itself, an analytical tool. I have written some version of that note every working week of my life since.
- An instinct, in any planning meeting, to ask whether the schedule on the page was the schedule the project actually had to keep, or merely the schedule the room had got used to looking at. A great many German workshops had taught me how much earlier a project can finish when somebody is willing to ask that question early.

That last instinct, more than any other, became the habit by which I think the rest of my career was shaped. I learned, in the cyanide and peroxide years, to give my management the dates I believed in rather than the dates the project plan made convenient, and then to do the work of making the dates I believed in stand up. The phrase that I came to use for it, much later, when younger engineers asked me about it, was that I had decided to be a man who under-promised and over-delivered. It was not a slogan. It was a working method. It said, in plain language, that I would rather walk into a board meeting with a finish line behind me than with a finish line still in front of me, and that I would do whatever the work required, in advance, to put myself on the right side of that line.

## What Germany Had Bought

I look back now, from the distance of forty years, on the German assignment and the two AECI plant turnarounds that followed it, and I see them as a single piece of work. They taught me, between them, the four things I had not learned at Sub Nigel or at Libanon or in the early Sasolburg years, and that I would draw on for the rest of my working life.

They taught me that a specification, properly defended, is the cheapest insurance any project can buy. They taught me that a contractor, properly persuaded, will do more for a project than any contract can compel him to do. They taught me that a plant, properly listened to, will tell its manager where its real bottlenecks live, and that those bottlenecks are almost never in the place its instruments are pointing. And they taught me that an engineer who is willing to commit, in advance, to a finish line earlier than the room expects, and then to do the small disciplined work that makes that finish line stand up, will, over a career, accumulate a particular kind of credibility that no qualification can substitute for.

I had gone to Germany as a plant manager from Sasolburg with three small children and a folder I had been told not to lose. I came back as an engineer with a working method. The folder, by then, was much thinner. The method, by then, was much harder to lose.

Antonette and the children settled back into the East Rand. The cyanide plant ran. The peroxide plant ran. The Friday notes went out. And somewhere in the head offices of a consumer-goods business in another part of the country, on a desk I had never seen, my name was beginning to be spoken in conversations I was not yet in the room for.

# Adcock Ingram: Fourteen Years That Built a Director

The advertisement, when I first read it, did not look like the start of anything. It was a Sunday newspaper, the kind that lay on the lounge floor of a great many East Rand houses on a Sunday afternoon, and I had picked it up out of the loose habit a working engineer has of turning past the news pages and looking, without really meaning to, at the small print of the appointments section at the back. I had no intention of leaving AECI. I had a cyanide plant that was running half as much again as it had been, a peroxide plant that had quietly followed the same path, a Sasolburg house that suited the children, and a head office in Johannesburg that had begun, in the careful language of large chemical companies, to indicate that I should not be in too much of a hurry about what came next.

I read the advertisement anyway. It was placed by a consumer-pharmaceutical company in Johannesburg, the kind of company whose products were on the shelves of every chemist on the East Rand, and it was looking for a Company Engineer. I noticed, as I read down the paragraph, that the role was a singular role. There was no plural in it. There was no department of engineers stacked underneath a chief. There was one engineer, with one telephone number on his desk, responsible for the engineering of the whole company. I had spent the previous several years inside an AECI engineering organisation in which I was one of fifteen. I closed the newspaper, folded it onto the side table, and went out into the small back garden to think about the difference between fifteen and one.

I did not, that Sunday, apply. I applied the following weekend, on what I told Antonette was a kind of curiosity rather than a kind of intention. I posted a single sheet of typed paper that gave my dates, my qualifications, my AECI assignments and nothing else, and I addressed it to the box number in the corner of the advertisement. I expected, in the way one expects when one applies casually to a singular advertisement, to hear nothing further.

## The Interview That Went Too Well

The reply came on a Tuesday. It came by telephone, through the AECI switchboard, and the secretary who put it through did so in the slightly puzzled tone she reserved for callers from outside the chemicals industry. The man on the other end of the line introduced himself in the unhurried language of a senior person who is accustomed to being heard out, named the company, named the role, and asked whether I would be willing to come into Johannesburg the following week for a conversation. I said yes, in the same way I would later, on another Tuesday and a different desk, say yes to a chairman from Port Elizabeth before I had finished saying hello. I had not yet learned, in those years, that the working answer to that kind of invitation is yes long before the conscious answer has been formed.

I drove in on a clear winter morning, in a suit that I had last worn to a wedding, and I sat in a reception area that smelled, faintly and unmistakably, of the kind of inks and adhesives that belong to a packaging operation rather than a chemical works. The interview, when it began, was not really an interview. It was a long, slow, carefully steered conversation, conducted across a desk by two men who had clearly read my single sheet of paper more carefully than I had expected, and who had decided in advance what they were going to ask. They asked me about Sasolburg. They asked me about the cyanide plant. They asked me about the Dusseldorf work, and they asked me, more particularly, about the way I had persuaded the German contractor to run nights. They were not testing the technical content of my answers. They were testing the shape of the engineer behind the answers.

I noticed, somewhere in the middle of the conversation, that the meeting had gone too well. The two men across the desk had stopped asking the kind of question that elicits information and had begun asking the kind of question that builds a picture of what a working week would look like if I were already inside the building. They asked me how I would handle a particular packing-hall situation. They asked me how I would talk to a particular kind of foreman. They asked me what I would do, in my first week, if I walked into a room of senior managers who had been running their own engineering by committee for a decade. I answered them, in the unguarded way one answers when one has not yet decided one is being interviewed for the job, and by the end of the morning the offer was effectively on the desk between us.

I drove back to Sasolburg in a different mood from the one in which I had driven in. I had not, on the Sunday I first read the advertisement, intended to leave AECI. I now had an offer, in everything but writing, that would do so.

## Declined, and Then Accepted

I declined the offer the following week. I did so in writing, in the careful language one uses when one is declining something one half wants, and I did so for reasons I had set out for myself the previous evening at the kitchen table with Antonette. The reasons were not strange. AECI had been good to me. The Sasolburg house was settled. The third child was in a school that suited her, and the fourth was not yet a thought we were allowing ourselves to entertain. The cyanide and peroxide plants were running on a method I had spent four years building, and the head office in Johannesburg had begun to talk, in the careful AECI way, about a future I had every reason to trust. I posted the letter on the Wednesday. I expected, on the Thursday, to feel relieved.

I did not. I felt, instead, the particular kind of unease that arrives when one has made a sensible decision against an instinct that has not yet found its words. I sat with it for a fortnight. I walked the cyanide plant on a Monday morning, and the peroxide plant on a Tuesday afternoon, and I wrote the Friday note to head office in the usual way, and I went home each evening to a house in which Antonette, who had read me longer than anyone else in my life, said nothing about the letter and waited for me to come round to the conversation in my own time.

The conversation, when we had it, was short. I had decided, somewhere in the second week of walking my plants and thinking about them differently, that I was being offered a role I had not asked for and had not previously imagined, and that the right answer to a role of that kind is not the sensible answer but the honest one. I had been a member of a fifteen-strong engineering organisation for long enough to know exactly what it would mean to become the only engineer in a building. It would mean being out of my depth in the morning and not being out of my depth by the evening, every day, for at least the first two years. It would mean a kind of accelerated stretching of the analytical method I had been quietly forming in the cyanide years. It would mean answering, alone, the question I had previously been able to share with fourteen other men.

I wrote, the following Monday, to the company in Johannesburg, and I asked, in the language one uses when one is asking a question one has no formal right to ask, whether the offer was still open. It was. I gave AECI my notice that week.

## The Resignation That Shocked AECI

The AECI senior people had not seen the resignation coming. I had taken some care, in the previous fortnight, to make sure they would not. I did not believe in announcing a decision before it had been made, and I did not believe in negotiating with my own employer about an offer that I had, formally, already declined. When I walked into my manager's office on the Wednesday morning with a single sheet of paper in my hand, he assumed, until he read the second paragraph, that I was bringing him a Friday note he had not been expecting on a Wednesday. He read the page twice. He looked up at me, and he asked, in the slightly unguarded voice of a senior man who has been blindsided in his own office, whether I had any idea what the company had been planning to do with me. I said that I did, and that I was grateful for it, and that I was leaving anyway.

There were the conversations one would expect. There was a meeting that afternoon, and a meeting the following morning, and a quiet drive across the works to a more senior office in which a more senior man asked the same question in a more measured language. I gave the same answer. I did not name the new company. I did not name the salary. I said only that I had been offered a role that I needed to take, that I was not negotiating, and that I would work my notice in full and leave the cyanide and peroxide plants in the state I had been keeping them in. They accepted it, in the end, with the kind of formal grace large chemical companies are good at, and I walked out of my last AECI Friday with the folder of Friday notes still on the desk for whoever came next.

I drove home that evening past the boundary fence of the Sasolburg works for what I knew was the last time as an AECI engineer. I have driven past it many times since. I have never looked at it without a quiet thankfulness for the four years it had given me, and for the analytical method it had let me form in conditions that, in retrospect, were almost laboratory-clean for the purpose.

## The Only Engineer in the Building

I started at Adcock Ingram on a Monday morning in the second half of that year. I was, as the advertisement had promised, the only engineer in the building. There was a maintenance organisation underneath me, run by foremen and fitters of long experience, and there were technical specialists scattered through the production and quality areas who knew their corners of the operation better than I did. But there was no senior engineer above me, and there was no peer engineer beside me, and there was no chief of department to whom I could refer the questions I did not yet know how to answer. The questions came to my desk. They stayed there until I had answered them.

I spent the first month walking. I walked the way the German months and the cyanide year had taught me to walk, which is to say I walked with the senior foreman on a Monday morning, with the quality manager on a Tuesday afternoon, with a packing-line operator on a Wednesday night, and with whichever maintenance fitter had been in the building the longest on a Thursday. I asked, in each of those walks, the same two questions I had learned to ask in Sasolburg. What was the plant being prevented from doing. What was being treated, in the room, as a permanent compromise that was in fact only the widow of a reason long since dead. The answers were different from the cyanide answers. They came in a different language, and they pointed at different bottlenecks, but the shape of the problem was familiar. A mature operation, settled into a fraction of its potential, with a quiet structure of inherited assumptions that nobody had recently asked to revisit.

I had walked into a different industry. I had not walked out of the method.

## Loss, and a Fourth Child

The personal weather of those first Adcock years was harder than the working weather. My father had died at sixty-three of the lung disease that had been working its slow way through him for most of the Libanon years. I had carried his death into AECI and through the German winter and through the cyanide turnaround, and I had thought, by the time I arrived at Adcock, that I had taken the measure of what it would mean to live without him. I had not. My mother, who had held the East Rand house together on a miner's wage for the better part of forty years, did not long survive him. She died a year after he did, at fifty-four, in a small bedroom in the same semi-detached mine house in which she had raised the five of us.

I do not propose to write the detail of that year. It belongs to the family rather than to a memoir, and the parts of it I would want to write are the parts I am least able to. I will say only that the discipline I had been forming, the analytical method, the Friday note, the walk through the plant on a Monday morning, became, in the months on either side of my mother's funeral, the scaffolding that held the working week together when the personal week was not yet able to. Antonette held the rest. She has always held the rest. There is no chapter of my working life that has not, in some quiet structural way, been a chapter of hers.

It was in the middle of that year, or perhaps the early part of the following one, that we found out that a fourth child was on the way. She arrived in due course, healthy and welcome, and she settled into the house in the way that a fourth child settles, which is to say with three older siblings already running the daily curriculum and a father who had begun, with her birth, to think more carefully than he ever had before about the time he was giving to a working week. I made some adjustments in those years. I did not make as many as I should have. I have written, more than once in this book, that the time owed to a family is not the same as the time that fits comfortably around an ambitious career, and that the gap between the two is a debt that has to be either paid in advance or repented of later. I paid some of it in advance. The rest I am still, in my own way, addressing.

## The Packing-Hall Redesign

The first substantial piece of engineering work I did at Adcock was the redesign of one of the company's packing halls. It is not a piece of work I will dwell on at length, because the pharmaceutical-manufacturing discipline that came with it deserves a chapter of its own, and will get one. But the bones of it were these. The hall, as I inherited it, had been laid out a long time previously, in conditions that no longer obtained. The product mix had drifted. The line speeds had crept up. The hygiene-zone boundaries had been redrawn, more than once, on top of the original walls. The result was a hall that worked, in the sense that product came out of one end of it on most shifts, but worked at a fraction of the throughput it could have carried and at a level of operator strain that, on the floor, I could feel within a fortnight.

I walked it. I sat with the line supervisors. I sat with the quality people. I drew, in the evenings at home on the kitchen table, sketch after sketch of a flow that would unwrap the loops the hall had been quietly tying around itself for years. I costed it as cheaply as I could, because I did not yet have the standing inside the building to ask for a great deal of capital. I presented it, in the end, to a board that did not, at first, quite know what to do with the only-engineer-in-the-building bringing them a redesign of a hall their longest-serving people had been running for fifteen years. They approved a phased version of it. We built the first phase over a long weekend, with the line stopped on the Friday afternoon and running again on the Tuesday morning, and the throughput lifted measurably on the same product mix and the same crew. The second and third phases followed within the year.

It was a small piece of work in the scale of the things that came afterwards. It was the piece of work that bought me, inside the building, the standing I would need for everything that came after it.

## Boston, and a Reverse-Osmosis Plant

The second substantial assignment of the early Adcock years took me overseas again. The company needed a reverse-osmosis water plant of pharmaceutical quality for one of its main manufacturing sites, and the technology, at that scale and to that specification, was best procured from a small specialist supplier in the Boston area. I was sent to negotiate it, inspect it on the supplier's floor, and accept it on behalf of the company. I flew, this time, alone, with a folder rather thicker than the German folder had been and with a different kind of nerve from the one I had borrowed in 1980.

Boston in those years was not Dusseldorf. The American supplier did business in a faster, more verbal way than the German contractor had done, and the specification meetings were shorter and louder and more inclined to settle, in the room, what the Germans had insisted on settling in writing. I adjusted my method. I kept the analytical core of it. I let the surface of it pick up the pace of the room. I asked the same questions I would have asked in Dusseldorf, but I asked them across a smaller table and in a different rhythm, and I wrote the same kind of single-page note to the Johannesburg head office on the same kind of Friday afternoon, because some habits, by then, were no longer adjustable.

I came home with a reverse-osmosis plant that performed, on commissioning, on the day the contract said it would. I came home, also, with a quiet confidence that the working method I had built in Sasolburg was not, as I had half feared, a German artefact. It was portable. It was the same method in Boston as it had been in Dusseldorf, and it would prove, over the years, to be the same method on every continent on which I would eventually have to use it.

## Germany Again, and a Sterile Machine

Some years into the Adcock work I was sent back to Germany. It was a different assignment in a different yard, on a different kind of equipment, but the country was the same and a great many of the working habits were the same, and I found myself, in the first week, falling back into the rhythms of the Dusseldorf winter with an ease that surprised me. The assignment, this time, was training on a piece of sterile-filling machinery that the company had bought for one of its higher-specification production halls. The training was technical in the narrow sense, and it was technical in the wider sense as well. The Germans were not only showing me how the machine worked. They were showing me how the machine had to be cared for, by whom, in what discipline, on what cadence, if it was to deliver the level of integrity the pharmaceutical world expected of it.

I made notes in the same small private notebook I had used at the cyanide plant. I came back to Adcock with a discipline of sterile manufacturing that we put into practice on the floor in Johannesburg, line by line, over the months that followed. The Germans had taught me, the first time, how to defend a specification. The Germans had taught me, this second time, how to defend a sterile boundary. The two lessons would, in due course, run together into the manufacturing instinct that the next chapter of this book belongs to.

## Bringing the Jeyes Line Home

Towards the middle of my Adcock years the company took on a transfer of production that did more to stretch my engineering than any single project before it. The Jeyes blue-line range, which the British parent had been producing at East Kilbride in Scotland for many years, was to be relocated to South Africa. The plant, in pieces, was to come down the Atlantic in shipping containers. The line was to be re-erected in a hall I had been preparing for it on the Johannesburg site. The product, on the other side of the transfer, was to come off the South African line in the same specification, in the same packaging, with the same shelf life, and at the same hygiene standard as it had been coming off the Scottish line for years.

I flew to East Kilbride more than once in the months leading up to the transfer. I walked the line in Scotland with the engineer who had been running it. I made the same kind of notes I had made in Dusseldorf and in Boston and in the second German yard. I asked the line's foremen, in the unhurried way one asks foremen who have run a line for a long time, what the line knew that the drawings did not. The answers, written down patiently over several visits, became the unwritten half of the transfer document. The written half was straightforward. The unwritten half was where the transfer would either succeed or fail.

We brought the line down in containers in the order I had specified. We re-erected it in the prepared hall in Johannesburg over a period of weeks that I had compressed, against the recommendation of the British engineers, by running parallel installation crews on the sub-assemblies I knew could be brought in independently. We commissioned the line in stages, with the Scottish engineer present for the first run and a South African crew running the second, and we made our first commercial batch of blue-line product on the South African floor within the window I had given the board. The product came off the line in the same specification it had come off the Scottish line in. The shelf-life trials, over the months that followed, confirmed that the transfer had not cost the product its integrity. The line, by the end of its first South African year, was running at a rate that compared favourably with its East Kilbride benchmark.

It was, by some distance, the most complex piece of engineering I had personally led to that point. It was also the piece of work that, inside the building, settled my standing for the years that followed.

## Technical Director

The promotion, when it came, did not come with a great deal of brass. It came in the unhurried language of a chairman in a quiet office, in a conversation that started with the Jeyes line and ended, somewhere in the second half of the morning, with the offer of the Technical Directorship of the Consumer Division. The role would put me on the operating board. It would give me responsibility for the whole supply chain of a division that ran several factories, dozens of lines, a procurement function whose reach extended into Europe and the United States, and a quality organisation that, in the pharmaceutical and consumer-pharmaceutical sense, carried consequences I had only begun, in the previous several years, to appreciate properly.

I accepted it. I went home, that evening, and I told Antonette over a kitchen table that had heard, by then, a great many of the working decisions of my life, that I had become a Director. She did what she always did when a working week brought home a piece of news of that scale. She thanked God for it, in the quiet language of her own faith, and she asked me what it would mean for the children. I told her, as honestly as I could, that it would mean more travel and longer days, and that I would do what I could to make sure the cost did not fall on the wrong side of the family. She nodded. She has always nodded when an honest answer has been offered, and she has always, in her own quiet way, held me to it.

I walked into the building the following Monday with the same notebook in my pocket, the same Friday discipline in my head, and a card on the office door that had, for the first time in my life, the word Director on it. The card surprised me, on the first morning, more than I expected it to. The work, mercifully, did not. The supply chain of a Consumer Division turned out to be, in its bones, a larger version of the kind of operation I had been analysing patiently since the cyanide years. The bottlenecks were in different places. The compromises were in different colours. The structure was the same. I walked the factories the way I had walked the packing hall. I wrote the Friday note the way I had written it from Sasolburg and from Dusseldorf and from Boston. I gave my people the dates I believed in rather than the dates the planning meetings made convenient.

## Fourteen Years, in the End

I look back now, from the distance of more than two decades, on the fourteen years I gave to Adcock Ingram, and I am still slightly surprised by how much of the working method I would carry for the rest of my career was finished in that building. I had arrived in the early thirties as the only engineer in the building. I left in the early forties as a Technical Director with the supply chain of a Consumer Division behind me, a fourth child at school, a wife who had held the home together through the German winters and the Boston weeks and the Scottish months, and a working method that had been tested in pharmaceuticals and not found wanting.

I will not, in this chapter, rehearse the pharmaceutical-manufacturing discipline that runs through everything I have just described. It deserves its own treatment, and it will have one. I will say only, by way of closing, that the fourteen years at Adcock did three things for me that I did not yet have when I walked in. They taught me to be the only engineer in a building, which is to say to be a man whose questions cannot be referred sideways and must be answered. They taught me to work in an industry whose consequences are quieter and longer and more unforgiving than the consequences of a heavy-chemicals plant. And they made me, by the end of the fourteenth year, ready in a way I did not yet have words for, for a telephone call I was not yet expecting, from a chairman I had not yet met, about a Managing Directorship in a city I had never lived in.

That call, when it came, would come on an ordinary afternoon. Antonette and I would sit at the kitchen table that evening with a decision of a different scale in front of us. The fourteen Adcock years would, in retrospect, look like a long and careful preparation for the question that telephone call put on the table. At the time, they had looked only like a working life.

existingChapters.summary: Explores the discipline of manufacturing products that can kill people if mishandled: validations, housekeeping, food and pharma safety standards, and the Japanese 'fool-proof' Poka-Yoke philosophy that became a permanent operating instinct. Uses Adcock Ingram as the case study to teach readers why mistake-prevention systems, FSSC accreditations and 5S principles are the real moat under any sustainable operation.

# Manufacturing Discipline: GMP, Poka-Yoke and Pharmaceutical Rigour

There is a particular kind of quiet that settles over a pharmaceutical packing hall in the small hours of the morning, when the lines are running and the operators have found their rhythm and the only sound left is the soft, regular percussion of bottles meeting their caps. It is not the quiet of a workshop after hours. It is the quiet of a place where, on every shift, men and women are making products that, if they leave the building in the wrong condition, can kill the person who eventually swallows them. I had not understood, when I left the cyanide and peroxide plants of AECI for the consumer-pharmaceuticals world of Adcock Ingram, that I was crossing into an industry whose first commandment was not productivity, and not even safety in the ordinary sense, but a discipline so much older and so much more unforgiving that it had its own three-letter shorthand: GMP.

Good Manufacturing Practice. Three words that sound, when you first hear them, like the kind of slogan a chairman puts on a calendar. Three words that, when you live inside them for fourteen years, become the operating instinct of every decision you make for the rest of your career.

## A different category of consequence

I had come into Adcock from a heavy-chemicals world in which the consequences of a mistake were dramatic, immediate and, in their own grim way, easy to understand. A reactor overpressured. A flange leaked. A trip closed the plant. The damage was loud and the diagnosis was honest. You could see it, you could smell it, and the trend chart on the control-room wall would tell you, after the fact, exactly when in the previous twenty-four hours the failure had begun to assemble itself.

Pharmaceutical manufacturing is not like that. The consequence of a mistake in a pharmaceutical plant can be invisible for weeks, or months, or in the worst cases years. A blister pack that has been overfilled by one tablet. A label that has been printed on the wrong reel of foil. A sterile fill that has lost integrity for ninety seconds inside an eight-hour shift. A solvent residue that is one part per million above its specification on a single batch out of eighty. None of those failures announces itself in the room they occur in. They announce themselves later, in a patient's bathroom, in a doctor's surgery, in a regulator's letter, or, on the days I never want to live through again, in a recall.

That is the category of consequence a pharmaceutical engineer is paid to prevent. And the moment you have understood that, you understand why GMP is not a paperwork exercise and not a slogan on a calendar. GMP is the institutional memory of every mistake the industry has ever made, written down in such a way that no plant has to make it twice.

## The discipline of writing it down

The first thing that struck me, in my early months as Company Engineer at Adcock, was how much of the work consisted of writing.

I had come from plants where the engineer's job was, in essence, to keep things running and to keep people safe while they did so. The writing happened, of course, but it happened around the work. In pharmaceuticals the writing was the work. Every procedure was documented. Every change was controlled. Every deviation was investigated. Every batch had a record that could be unrolled, years later, in front of an auditor, and that had to tell, line by signed line, the entire story of how a particular bottle of cough syrup had come to be a bottle of cough syrup.

I will admit that, in the beginning, it irritated me. I had grown up on the principle that an engineer earns his stripes on the plant floor and not at a desk, and the sheer volume of paper that pharmaceutical manufacture demanded felt, for a while, like a tax on real work. It took me a year of audits and investigations and one or two genuinely frightening near-misses to understand that the paper was not a tax on the work. The paper was the work. An undocumented change in a pharmaceutical plant is not a change. It is an unauthorised alteration of a product that human beings are about to put inside their bodies, and the discipline of writing it down is the discipline that separates a manufacturer from an experimenter.

Once I had absorbed that, the writing stopped irritating me. It started teaching me. The change-control log of a well-run pharmaceutical plant is, if you read it patiently, a textbook on how the plant actually thinks. The deviation register is a textbook on where its weaknesses are. The batch records are a textbook on the consistency, or otherwise, of the people running the lines. I learned, in those Adcock years, that an engineer who cannot read his own plant's paperwork is an engineer who is, in the end, working in someone else's plant.

## Validation: proving the obvious

The other shock of those first months was validation.

In a chemicals plant, you commission a unit, you run it, you tune it, and you move on. In a pharmaceutical plant, you validate it. You prove, with documented evidence, that the equipment does what you say it does, that the process produces what you say it produces, and that the product meets specification not once but consistently, batch after batch, under the conditions you have committed to. You do this before the first commercial batch leaves the door. You do it again whenever anything material changes. And you do it, when you are honest with yourself, with a small part of your mind constantly asking whether the next inspector is going to find something you did not.

The first validation exercise I led at Adcock was a packing-line redesign that, in any other industry, would have been signed off in an afternoon. In pharmaceuticals it took the better part of three months. I had to write the protocol. I had to argue, line by line, why each acceptance criterion was the right one. I had to run the line under nominal conditions, under stressed conditions, and under the worst-case combination of conditions the protocol could justify. I had to record every reading, sign every page, and explain every anomaly. By the end of it I had a fat lever-arch file, a vague sense that I had aged a year in a quarter, and an understanding of validation that no textbook could have given me.

Validation, I came to see, is simply the engineer's discipline of proving the obvious. It is the formal admission that the obvious, in a regulated industry, is not allowed to remain merely obvious. It has to be demonstrated, documented and defended. And the moment you accept that as a worldview, your relationship with your own plant changes. You stop trusting it. You start verifying it. And, paradoxically, you start running it better than you ever did when you trusted it.

## Housekeeping as evidence

There is a corollary to that worldview which I have carried into every plant I have run since, and it is this. In a pharmaceutical or food plant, housekeeping is not housekeeping. Housekeeping is evidence.

A regulator who walks into a packing hall and sees clean floors, marked walkways, labelled bins, dated stock, sequenced product changeovers and operators in clean PPE does not, in the first instance, congratulate the plant on its tidiness. He registers, in the part of his brain that is paid to register such things, that the plant is being run by people who understand what they are doing. Conversely, a regulator who walks into a hall and sees scattered offcuts, mixed allergens on adjacent lines, undated work-in-progress and labels stored on the floor does not, in the first instance, write the plant up for poor housekeeping. He registers that the plant is, in his professional judgement, an outbreak waiting to happen, and the rest of his audit is conducted from that assumption.

That was the lesson I absorbed at Adcock, and it is the lesson I took, line for line, into every food and consumer plant I subsequently ran. The 5S markings on the floor of a beverage plant. The colour-coded scoops in a peanut-butter line. The sterile zones, the high-care zones, the allergen segregation. The dated tape on the inside of a tank. The mop that has its own labelled hook, and a different colour for the cleaning of a different zone. None of these are aesthetic decisions. They are the visible vocabulary of a manufacturer telling its regulators, its customers and, most importantly, itself, that it knows the difference between a clean line and a compliant one.

## Poka-Yoke: the Japanese gift

The phrase I have used more often than any other in the second half of my career came to me, indirectly, from the Japanese.

Poka-Yoke. Mistake-proofing. The principle that a process should be designed in such a way that the wrong action cannot, physically or procedurally, be taken. A connector that only fits one way. A label that only feeds in one orientation. A bottle that cannot pass a fill checkweigher unless it is filled to a tolerance the line has already accepted. A door that cannot be opened during a high-care cycle without halting the line. A code on a carton that cannot be over-printed unless the master recipe and the printer's instruction agree.

The genius of Poka-Yoke, I have come to believe, is not its cleverness. The genius is its humility. It begins from the engineer's acceptance that human beings, including very good ones, including ones who have been on the line for twenty years, will, under fatigue or distraction or unusual conditions, sooner or later do the wrong thing. The instinctive Western engineering response to that fact is to train, to retrain, to discipline and, in due course, to blame. The Japanese response is older and wiser. It is to design the wrong thing out of the system, so that the line cannot make the mistake even if the operator, on his worst day, attempts it.

I learned Poka-Yoke first at Adcock, in the context of pharmaceutical packing, where the consequences of a label or batch-code error are very serious indeed. But it did not stay in pharmaceuticals. It became, over the decades that followed, the single principle I carried into every plant I ran, in every industry I touched. The mogul lines, the chocolate plants, the peanut-butter operation, the beverage filling halls, the food-supplement plant — every one of them was, at some level, a project to embed Poka-Yoke in the place where, before I arrived, training and exhortation had been doing the work that geometry and interlocks should have been doing.

That habit, I should say plainly, is what FSSC accreditation, BRC certification, and the rest of the food-safety alphabet are really measuring. They are not measuring how clean the plant looks on audit day. They are measuring whether the plant has been designed, in detail, so that the wrong outcome cannot happen on the day no auditor is present.

## The pharmaceutical mindset, ported to food

When I left Adcock, fourteen years later, and began to run food and consumer plants in the years that followed, I took the pharmaceutical mindset with me intact. I have sometimes been asked, in interviews and in classrooms, whether it is reasonable to apply pharmaceutical disciplines to a peanut-butter line or a sweet-manufacturing operation. My answer has not changed in three decades. It is not only reasonable. It is the cheapest insurance policy a food operator can buy.

A consumer who buys a jar of peanut butter does not read the batch record. She trusts, in the casual way consumers trust, that the jar in her trolley is what its label says it is, that it contains what its ingredients list says it contains, and that nothing in it will make her child ill. That trust is the entire commercial premise of the brand. And the only mechanism on earth that protects that trust, in the long run, is a manufacturing discipline that treats the jar with the same seriousness a pharmaceutical company treats a tablet.

In practice that meant several specific things, and I list them here not as theory but as the working principles I imported, plant after plant, into operations that had often never seen them.

- Every critical process step had to have a documented procedure, signed off, version-controlled, and present, in laminated form, at the station where the step was performed. If the procedure was not at the station, the step did not exist.
- Every change to a process, however small, had to pass through a written change-control protocol. The line operator who tightens a screw on his own initiative is, in a regulated environment, an unmanaged risk. The change-control register is what converts that initiative into a managed improvement.
- Every deviation, however apparently trivial, had to be investigated to root cause and closed out with a documented corrective and preventive action. A deviation closed with the words "operator error" is a deviation that has not been investigated. People are almost never the root cause. The system that allowed the person to fail is.
- Every line had to be designed, wherever the budget allowed, with Poka-Yoke at the level of geometry rather than at the level of attention. Where geometry could not deliver it, sensors and interlocks had to. Where sensors and interlocks could not deliver it, the procedure had to be written in such a way that the wrong action triggered an audible, witnessed stop.
- Every plant had to be walked, by its accountable manager, in the first hour of every shift. Not the morning shift. Every shift. The plant tells you what is going wrong with it long before the trend chart does, and the manager who is not on his lines at the start of each shift is, in my experience, the manager who is going to read about his own failures in a recall letter.

Those five principles are not, in themselves, novel. They are the everyday vocabulary of any decently run pharmaceutical operation. What was novel, in my career, was the patient business of carrying them across the industry boundary, into food and consumer manufacture, and demonstrating that they paid their own way in lower wastage, lower recall risk, higher OEE and a reputation with customers that no marketing budget could have bought.

## What the auditors really came for

In the Adcock years I sat through a great many audits. Internal audits, corporate audits, customer audits, regulator audits. The auditors changed. The questions did not.

What they came to see, underneath the paperwork, was very simple. They came to see whether the plant was being run by people who were honest about its weaknesses. A plant that pretends, on audit day, to have no deviations is a plant that is lying. A plant that hands the auditor a deviation register with one hundred and thirty entries, every one investigated, every one closed, every one trending toward a root cause that the plant is actively engineering out, is a plant whose auditor goes home satisfied.

I learned, in those years, to welcome auditors. I learned to give them the worst of my deviation register first, before they asked, and to walk them through what we had learned from each one. I learned that the manufacturer who tries to hide his weaknesses from a competent auditor is a manufacturer who is going to be found out, sooner or later, by a less forgiving instrument than an auditor's report. The market. The regulator. The recall. In that order of severity.

The habit of honesty about one's own plant, I have come to think, is the real moat under any sustainable manufacturing operation. Everything else — the equipment, the recipes, the people, the certifications — can be bought, copied or imitated. The discipline of looking, week after week, at one's own deviation register and asking what it is trying to teach is the one thing competitors cannot reverse-engineer.

## A permanent operating instinct

I left Adcock, in due course, for the next chapter of my working life, and I have written elsewhere about what came next. What I want to record here, while the subject is on the table, is what those fourteen years gave me that has never left.

They gave me a permanent operating instinct. The instinct to walk a plant looking for what is not quite right rather than for what is going well. The instinct to read a deviation register before I read a production report. The instinct to ask, of every new line, where its Poka-Yoke is and what it is doing on the day the most experienced operator is on leave. The instinct to treat housekeeping as evidence. The instinct to treat validation as the formal proof of the obvious. The instinct to be, in front of any auditor, a great deal more honest about my plant's weaknesses than the auditor was likely to have expected.

That instinct, I want to say plainly to any younger engineer who happens to be reading this, is not a personality trait. It is not a gift. It is the residue of fourteen years spent inside an industry that would not let me make a single decision casually, and it can be acquired by anyone who is willing to serve the same apprenticeship. You do not have to spend your career in pharmaceuticals to inherit it. You only have to spend long enough inside a properly regulated operation to understand, in your bones, that the price of consumer trust is paid in advance, in the small disciplines of attention, and never in arrears, in the loud heroics of recall management.

I had paid that price, by the end of my Adcock years, in a currency the rest of my career would continue to draw on. And when, some time later, a chairman in Port Elizabeth picked up the telephone with an offer that would change the shape of my working life again, the engineer he was calling was not the Engineer-in-Training who had once stood under the headgear at Sub Nigel. He was a manufacturing man, formed by GMP, fluent in Poka-Yoke, allergic to undocumented change, and quietly certain that the discipline of running a regulated plant was the discipline that would run anything.

existingChapters.title: Apprentice to Managing Director: Kolosus and Port Elizabeth
existingChapters.summary: Recounts being headhunted at forty-two by the chairman of a large export group and stepping into the Managing Director seat at Kolosus Holdings in Port Elizabeth, leaving two grown children behind in Johannesburg. Covers exports of Dorper sheep-skin leather to Italy, France, Japan, Malaysia and Europe, the twice-yearly Paris leather fairs, global negotiation lessons, the eventual sale of the company to a larger competitor, and the severance that closed a five-year chapter and returned the family to Gauteng.
