# Apprentice to Managing Director: Kolosus and Port Elizabeth

The telephone call came on an ordinary afternoon. I was at my desk at Adcock Ingram, with the kind of paperwork in front of me that a Technical Director acquires the way a workshop acquires sawdust, when a secretary I did not know put through a chairman I did not yet work for. He introduced himself, named his group, named the company inside that group whose Managing Director's seat had become vacant, and asked, in the unhurried way that confident men ask important questions, whether I would consider flying down to Port Elizabeth to meet him for a conversation.

I was forty-two years old. I had given fourteen years to Adcock. I had two grown children settled in Johannesburg, two younger children still at home with Antonette, a tennis court in the back garden, a squash ladder I was climbing every Tuesday evening, and a working week so thoroughly run-in that I could have driven the next decade of it with my eyes shut.

I said yes to the meeting before I had finished saying hello.

That, in itself, taught me something I have since written down for younger engineers. The career-defining call does not announce itself with brass. It comes on an ordinary afternoon, through an ordinary switchboard, from somebody you have never met, and your answer has to be ready before you have had time to be flattered by the question. Mine was. I had been quietly ready, in a way I did not yet have words for, for several years.

## The chairman, the headhunt and the seat

The group that wanted to see me was a large South African export house, and the company they wanted me to run was Kolosus Holdings. The chairman did not waste a meeting. He told me, over a coffee that neither of us drank, that he had been watching the manufacturing turnaround work I had signed my name to inside Adcock, that he had had me discreetly checked out with people whose opinions he trusted, and that he believed the Kolosus operation needed exactly the kind of engineer who treated a plant as a discipline before he treated it as a business.

I asked him three questions. What was the current state of the operation. What had been tried and not worked. And what, in his own view, was the single most uncomfortable truth on the company's balance sheet. He answered all three without hedging. The plant was tired. Several attempts at re-energising it had stalled because each had been built around a personality rather than a process. And the most uncomfortable truth was that the operation, despite being export-led in a market where the rand worked in our favour, was no longer earning the cost of capital his board had committed to it.

I have been in many interviews, on both sides of the table, over the decades since. A great many of them have been theatre. That one was not. By the time the coffee was cold I had been offered the seat. By the time my flight had landed back in Johannesburg that evening I had decided, in principle, that I would take it.

The harder conversation was still ahead. It was the one I had to have at the kitchen table.

## The kitchen-table conversation

A Managing Director's seat in Port Elizabeth, for a Johannesburg family with two grown children whose lives had already begun to root in Gauteng, is not a job offer. It is a relocation request that happens to come with a salary.

I laid it out for Antonette as honestly as I knew how. The opportunity was a step I had been preparing for since the morning I first walked through the Adcock gate. The package was generous. The chairman was a man I could work for. The plant was a challenge in the exact shape I had been trained, by GMP and by fourteen years of regulated manufacture, to take on. And the cost was specific. The two older children, settled in Johannesburg, would not be coming with us. The two younger children would come, and would have to be enrolled in schools in a city neither of us knew. Antonette would have to leave the tennis court, the church congregation and the small, settled rhythm of a household she had spent more than a decade building.

I said all of that and then stopped talking. The decisions in our marriage that have mattered have never been won by argument. They have been arrived at by the quieter process of two people listening to the same question until the same answer surfaces in both of them. We sat with the silence for a long time. Antonette, in the end, said only that if I felt this was the next step, she would make a home in Port Elizabeth as quickly and as completely as she had made every previous home. The faith she carried did not need a long speech to do its work. It simply re-centred the household around what would have to be done next.

I have written, in this book, about the foundations under a career. The Port Elizabeth move was the first time in our marriage that the foundation was tested by a relocation that left some of our children behind. It held. It held because Antonette refused, then as in every season afterwards, to let the family's centre be a postal address. The centre was the faith we shared, and the faith we shared travels light.

## Walking into Kolosus

I arrived at Kolosus in the same way I have arrived at every operation since. Quietly. Without a speech. Without a hundred-day plan announced from a podium. I had learned, by my early forties, that the new Managing Director who walks in and tries to impose himself on the operation in his first week is the Managing Director the floor adopts as an outsider for the rest of his tenure.

For the first fortnight I did not write a single email of substance. I did not make a single promise. I did not propose a single capital project. I walked. I stood next to the tanning drums. I watched a shaving machine for an hour without speaking. I read the maintenance log book before I read the management accounts. I asked the artisans, not the office, what was going wrong with the operation, and I asked them on the floor rather than in a meeting room, in front of the equipment that they would have to live with after I had gone home.

The plant told me what every tired plant tells the engineer who is willing to look. It told me that the operating disciplines had drifted. Procedures existed on paper but were not present at the stations where the work was done. Deviations were closed with "operator error" rather than investigated to root cause. Yield variation that should have been monitored by the hour was being noticed only by the month. Housekeeping had slipped from a daily standard to a Friday standard, and a plant whose floor is only clean on Fridays is a plant whose books are only honest on Fridays.

None of this was anybody's fault in particular. It was the steady, almost invisible erosion that overtakes a plant whose managers have stopped walking it at the start of every shift. I had seen the pattern at Adcock when I first joined as Company Engineer. I had seen it at AECI in Sasolburg. I would see it again, in different costume, at SA Oil Mills, at the confectionery sites, at Black Cat, at Future Life. The pattern is always the same. The remedy is always the same. The only variable is how long the new Managing Director is willing to wait before he insists on it.

## Dorper leather, and the customer behind every hide

Kolosus tanned and finished Dorper sheep-skin leather, and the Dorper is a peculiar gift of the South African veld. It is a hardy meat sheep, raised across the dry interior, and the skin it leaves behind is a remarkable raw material — fine-grained, durable, soft enough to take a high-fashion finish, tough enough to satisfy buyers who would not normally look south of the equator for premium leather. We exported it to Italy, to France, to Japan, to Malaysia, into the broader European market and into one or two niche corners of South-East Asia that I had not previously had to find on a map.

The customer behind every hide changed the way I thought about the plant. In pharmaceuticals, the customer is, in the end, a regulator and a pharmacist standing between the product and a patient. In leather for the European fashion houses, the customer is a buyer who has spent his career running the back of his hand across other people's hides. He can feel a tannage error from across a room. He knows, before he has asked you a price, whether your plant is being run by people who care about what they are sending him.

That changed my walking routine. I walked the wet finishing department the way I had walked the validation suite at Adcock. I asked, of every stage in the process, what the Italian buyer's hand would notice when the consignment landed in Solofra or Vicenza three weeks later. The discipline of writing things down, the procedural rigour I had imported from pharmaceuticals, fitted onto a tannery floor more naturally than I had expected. The product was different. The principle was not.

## Twice a year in Paris

Twice a year, I flew to Paris for the leather fairs. They were, and remain, the marketplaces in which the world's leather industry decides what next season's handbags, jackets, shoes and upholstery are going to look like. The buyers come. The tanners exhibit. The negotiations happen in a dozen languages around small stands with cuttings of finished hides hanging like fabric swatches in a haberdashery.

The first fair was an education in humility. I had spent twenty-six years inside South African manufacturing, and I had imagined that I knew what tough negotiation looked like. The Italian leather buyers taught me, inside half a morning, that I had been negotiating in the shallow end. They walked onto a stand with a complete picture of the global price book in their heads. They knew what the Argentine and Turkish tanneries were quoting. They knew what the dollar had done overnight. They had a position before they had a hello.

I learned a great deal in those Paris weeks that I have carried into every export negotiation since. Four lessons in particular.

- **Walk in with two prices.** The price you want, and the price you would still walk away from happily. If you do not have the second price in your head before the meeting begins, you have already lost margin you did not know you were giving away.
- **Do not be moved by the room.** The fair is theatre. The hospitality is theatre. The man who wines you in the evening is the same man who will quote you a number in the morning that pretends the evening never happened. Warm hospitality is a wonderful thing, but it does not pay the freight bill back to Port Elizabeth.
- **Know what the buyer is going to do with the hide.** A buyer who is finishing the leather for a shoe upper does not value the same things in a hide as a buyer who is cutting it for a jacket panel. If you know his finished product better than he expects you to, the conversation changes.
- **Honour every commitment, especially the small ones.** The fashion industry talks to itself. A South African supplier who delivers a sample two days early, in the exact specification, in the exact volume, will be on three buyers' shortlists by the next fair. A supplier who is late once will be off all three.

The fairs were also, quietly, an education in geography. I had been raised on the East Rand. I had worked in Sasolburg and Johannesburg and now Port Elizabeth. Paris twice a year, with side trips to the Italian tanning districts and occasional flights onward to Japan and Malaysia for customer visits, slowly knocked the parochialism out of my view of the world. South Africa, I came to understand, was a small country with a big plant inventory, and the operating disciplines that worked in our Port Elizabeth tannery worked in Solofra and in Osaka too. The product changed. The machine did not.

## Antonette in Port Elizabeth

While I was on aeroplanes, Antonette built the household in Port Elizabeth from scratch. New schools for the younger children. A new congregation. A new street, a new butchery, a new doctor, a new electrician. The two older children stayed in Johannesburg, and the weekend telephone call became the new substitute for the kitchen table. We flew them down for school holidays. We flew up to see them whenever the calendar allowed. The geographical seam in the family was the price the move had asked, and it was paid every weekend, in airline tickets and in the small ache of a father who was not down the corridor when his older two needed him.

I will say plainly here what I have said in other chapters in other words. The Port Elizabeth years were possible because Antonette made them possible. The schools were chosen, the congregation joined, the household rebuilt, the children's adjustment shepherded, by a woman whose centre was not her postcode. I came home to a settled house at the end of every trip because she had built one. I never had to manage the household and the plant simultaneously, because she managed the household completely while I managed the plant.

## The five years that taught me a Managing Director's job

The Managing Director's job, I learned at Kolosus, is not the same job as the Technical Director's job I had left at Adcock. The Technical Director answers for the plant. The Managing Director answers for the company. The plant is one of his ledgers. The customers are another. The exchange rate is a third. The workforce is a fourth. The board is a fifth. The chairman, if he is a good one, is a sixth. The community in which the company is rooted — in our case, a Port Elizabeth that depended on a handful of large employers — is a seventh.

I made my share of mistakes in those first eighteen months. I underestimated, in the early days, how much of the Managing Director's week had to be spent in the office of the chairman of an important customer rather than on the floor of his own plant. I underestimated, too, how much capital the operating improvements would require before they paid back, and I had one or two uncomfortable conversations with my own board explaining why a tannery cannot be turned around inside a single financial year.

But the discipline I had paid for, in fourteen Adcock years, did its work. The procedures came back to the stations. The deviation register came back to life. The shift-start walk-through came back into the manager's diary. The OEE numbers stopped being a monthly fiction and started being an hourly fact. The yield variation began to narrow. The Italian buyers, slowly, stopped asking me about quality and started asking me about volume. That, in any export business, is the inflection point you are looking for. Quality questions are the questions of a buyer who is testing you. Volume questions are the questions of a buyer who has already decided.

By the third year, we were a different company. By the fourth, we were a company that other companies in the group were beginning to look at with the appraising eye that precedes a corporate event. I had seen that eye before, in other industries. I knew what it usually meant.

## The sale, the severance and the road back to Gauteng

In the fifth year a larger competitor came forward with an offer for Kolosus that the chairman, after the appropriate process, could not in conscience recommend that his board refuse. The numbers worked. The strategic fit was clean. The acquirer's plans for the operation were, on paper, an extension of the work we had done rather than a reversal of it.

I had been in business long enough by then to know what an acquisition does to a Managing Director. The seat is the first piece of furniture the acquirer rearranges. The chairman explained it to me with the directness I had come to expect from him. The deal was good for the shareholders, good for the group and good, in the medium term, for Port Elizabeth. It was, in the immediate term, the closing of a chapter for me. The severance offered was fair. The handover would be orderly. The acquirer's team would be on site within the quarter.

I do not pretend that the news arrived without weight. Five years is a long stretch of a working life. The plant had become the plant my hands knew. The workforce had become the workforce whose family stories I knew at the gate. The Italian buyers had become customers I could read across a stand. Walking away from all of that, on somebody else's timetable, is not a thing an engineer does easily.

But I had also been taught, by the same fourteen years of GMP discipline, that the orderly close of an operation is its own form of professional respect. You do not protest the corporate event. You execute the handover. You write down what the new team will need to know. You walk the acquirer's engineers through the tannage protocols, the yield history, the customer contact list, the open issues on the deviation register, the small unspoken understandings with the chairmen of the customer houses in Solofra and Vicenza. You leave the operation in a condition that any successor can pick up.

I did that, and then I went home. The severance closed five years of my working life and a chapter of the family's life that none of us had quite expected to be temporary when it began. Antonette packed the household with the same quiet competence with which she had unpacked it. The younger children, by then more settled in Port Elizabeth than they had been in Johannesburg, were given the news as honestly as we knew how to give it. The two older children, still in Gauteng, would have us back within driving distance.

The drive north, when it came, was a quieter drive than the one south had been five years before. I had gone down a Technical Director and come back a Managing Director. I had gone down a domestic manufacturer and come back a global one. I had gone down a man whose toughest negotiations had been with regulators in Pretoria and come back a man whose toughest negotiations had been with leather buyers in Paris. The Sub Nigel apprentice who had once been told that boys from mine houses did not run companies was, by then, somebody else entirely.

There were offers waiting on the kitchen table in Gauteng. I sat down with Antonette and began the next sorting. But that, as the chairman had once said to me about a different decision, is a story for another conversation.

existingChapters.title: Transforming SA Oil Mills: The First Blueprint
existingChapters.summary: Tells the SA Oil Mills story as the origin of his business-transformation identity: the Tiger Brands and Senwes 50/50 joint venture, the two-week 'blue paper' that recommended rationalising duplicate crushing, refining and packing operations down to one of each, the R10-million automation capex, the ethical retrenchment process, the successful exit to Continental Oils, and the realisation that he could now walk into any business and see the big picture in days rather than months.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Transforming SA Oil Mills: The First Blueprint

There is a particular moment in a career when a habit you have been practising for thirty years quietly turns into a method. You do not notice it on the day it happens. You notice it later, sitting on a flight home or walking out of a board meeting that has gone unusually well, and you realise that the thing you used to feel your way through, plant by plant and problem by problem, has organised itself, without your asking, into something repeatable. SA Oil Mills was that moment for me. It was not the largest assignment of my working life, and it was not the most lucrative. It was the one in which the playbook I had been assembling, piece by piece since the cyanide plants of AECI and the validation files of Adcock Ingram, finally became a blueprint I could read aloud.

I came to it sideways. The severance from Kolosus had returned the family to Gauteng. I had spent a few weeks doing what every transformation man does between contracts — drinking tea I had not had time to drink for years, walking the dog of an afternoon, and waiting, with a discipline I had learned from Antonette more than from any of my employers, for the right phone call rather than the next phone call. The right call came from a senior man inside the Tiger Brands group. There was a joint venture, he said, that needed someone who could see a business plainly and act on what he saw. Tiger Brands and Senwes had taken equal shares in an edible-oils operation called SA Oil Mills. The asset was respectable on paper. The economics were not. Would I come and meet the board.

I went and met them. By the end of that morning I had agreed, in principle, to take responsibility for the operation, and I had agreed, more importantly, on the terms by which I would be measured. The two shareholders wanted a transformation, but they also wanted a clean answer to a question neither of them had quite formulated yet. Was this asset, in its current shape, worth keeping inside their respective portfolios at all. My job was to make it good enough that the question could be answered honestly, in either direction, on a foundation of operational reality rather than on the rumours of a tired plant.

## The Two-Week Blue Paper

I have a habit I have followed at every company I have ever joined, and I have written about it elsewhere in this book. In the first two weeks I do not write a single email asking anyone for a presentation. I do not call for the strategy decks that the previous management team prepared. I do not sit in the office reading historical board minutes, although I will read them later. I walk. I stand on the line next to the operators. I watch a crushing press for an hour without speaking. I count the small stoppages, the micro-changeovers, the quiet inefficiencies that nobody on the original team has noticed because they have been looking at them for so long. I have lunch in the canteen with the maintenance fitters. I read the breakdown reports. I walk the warehouse. I drive the perimeter of the property.

I gave myself two weeks to do that at SA Oil Mills, and at the end of the two weeks I wrote what I have called, ever since, a blue paper. The colour is not symbolic. The original document was simply printed on the blue stock of a printer that had been left in the corner of the office I had inherited, and the name stuck. A blue paper, in my private vocabulary, is the document a transformation man writes to himself first and to his shareholders second. It tells the truth about what is in front of him, in language a board can understand, without any of the dressing that consultants are paid to add. It is short. It is honest. It is testable.

The blue paper for SA Oil Mills, when I had written it, ran to no more than a dozen pages. It said, in summary, what the previous teams had not been willing to say in print. The operation was carrying three sets of nearly everything. Three crushing routes for the seed coming in. Two refining streams that did very similar work to one another. Multiple packing lines arranged for a product mix that had not, in truth, existed for several years. The fixed cost of running all of that duplicated capacity was draining the contribution of the variable margin. The plant was not failing because the people were poor — the people were, as it happened, the best part of the operation. The plant was failing because nobody had ever been given the authority, or the courage, to rationalise its own footprint down to one of each.

That was the central recommendation of the blue paper. One crushing line. One refining stream. One packing flow. The rest mothballed, sold, or repurposed, in that order of preference. Around the rationalisation, a focused capital programme of about ten million rand to automate the lines that would remain, so that the labour-intensive workarounds the operation had been living on for years could be removed at the same time as the duplicate capacity was taken out. And around the capital programme, an ethical retrenchment process, written out in detail before the first letter was signed, that would honour the people whose jobs the rationalisation was going to remove.

I took the blue paper to the joint shareholders' meeting two weeks and one day after I had walked through the gate. I read it to them aloud. I answered their questions for an hour. They gave me the mandate.

## Rationalising Down to One of Each

The work that followed was not, in its mechanics, exotic. It was the same work I had done in smaller doses at Adcock Ingram and in different forms at Kolosus, applied now to an operation that needed it across every step of its value chain at once.

We chose the best of the crushing lines and committed to it. The decision was not a beauty contest. We measured each line on a small set of variables that mattered — yield off the seed, reliability over the previous twenty-four months read from the breakdown reports, energy consumption per ton, and the cost and feasibility of bringing it up to the standard we wanted to operate at once the duplicates were gone. The line that scored best on that combined picture became the line we would run. The other two were stopped, in sequence, on dates we wrote into the project plan and held to.

The refining decision followed the same logic, and the packing flow followed it again. There is no point, in a thin-margin business, in keeping two pieces of capital running because the organisation is sentimental about a particular machine, or because a particular supervisor has spent his career on a particular line. The kindest thing a leader can do, in that situation, is to choose quickly, communicate clearly, and not let the decision drift. Drift is what destroys morale. Decision, properly explained, is what restores it.

While the rationalisation was being executed on the shop floor, I was running, in parallel, the capital case for the automation. The R10-million capex was modest by the standards of the projects I had run at Adcock Ingram and would later run at Tiger Brands, but every rand of it had to earn its return on a clear payback. We targeted the steps where the labour-intensive workarounds were doing the most damage — the manual handling on the front of the packing flow, the changeover routines that were eating an hour off every shift, the inspection points where a missed defect was going straight into a consumer pack. I specified the equipment myself. I procured it the way I had been taught to procure equipment at AECI, with a tender process that put the suppliers on the same page about what we needed and asked them to compete on engineering rather than on the size of the lunch they could buy us. We installed the new equipment in scheduled outages that did not lose the operation a single day of production it could not afford to lose.

The automation, when it was commissioned, did exactly what the business case had said it would. The hours-per-ton came down. The reject rate on the packing flow came down with it. The single crushing line, freed of the burden of running two of its siblings alongside it, ran at the kind of utilisation a board could be proud of. The single refining stream did the same. The operation, six months after the blue paper had been read aloud, looked like a different business.

## The Ethical Retrenchment

I want to write carefully about the retrenchment process, because it was the most important piece of the whole transformation, and it is the piece I am, even now, the most careful to do justice to.

A rationalisation of the kind I had recommended in the blue paper cannot be done without a reduction in headcount. To pretend otherwise — to claim, in the language some restructurings use, that there will be no impact on people, or that affected staff will be redeployed into roles that do not in fact exist — is a particular form of dishonesty that I have refused to participate in throughout my career. If you are going to take out a crushing line, you are going to take out the jobs that ran it. The honest question is not whether the impact exists, but how the impact is handled.

We handled it the way I have insisted on handling it at every operation I have ever led. We wrote the entire process down before we acted. We engaged the unions and the shop stewards at the start of the project rather than at the end. We costed an enhanced separation package that was meaningfully better than the legal minimum, on the basis that the operation could afford it and that the people who had built that operation deserved it. We set up retraining and outplacement support for those who wanted it. We offered, where the skills allowed, internal redeployment into the roles that the remaining capacity would still need. We gave realistic notice, not the kind of compressed notice that simply leaves families with a shorter runway. And I made a point, as I have made the same point at every operation since, of being personally present in the room when the difficult conversations happened. The man who has authorised the retrenchment should not, in my view, be at his desk while another manager carries the letter.

There were people on that site who, despite all of that preparation, found the news very hard. I want to be honest about that too. An ethical retrenchment is not a comfortable retrenchment. It is one in which the discomfort is at least carried by the right people, in the right order, with the right respect. Several of the men and women who left the site that quarter wrote to me afterwards. A few asked for personal references, which I gave without hesitation, having always held the view that a person who has worked well for me does not deserve a lesser reference because the role they worked in has been engineered out from underneath them. One or two of those references opened doors at other operations in the same town. I think of those letters, on the days I find myself doubting whether the hard decisions of a transformation life have been worth the weight they have carried, more often than I think of the savings the rationalisation delivered.

The retrenchment process, when it was complete, was audited by both shareholders. It passed without a single legal challenge. It passed, more importantly, without a single piece of the local resentment that a careless retrenchment leaves behind for years. The plant that emerged on the other side of the process was smaller in headcount than the one I had walked into. It was also, and I say this without sentimentality, a healthier place to work.

## The Exit to Continental Oils

By the time the rationalisation was bedded in, the automation was running, and the financials had spent two consecutive quarters in territory that the board could read without flinching, the question I had been hired to answer had answered itself. The asset was worth keeping inside somebody's portfolio. It was not, however, obvious that the somebody had to be the Tiger Brands and Senwes joint venture. Edible oils, as a category, sat awkwardly between the food-brands logic that Tiger Brands was built on and the grain-handling logic that Senwes was built on. The strategic fit on both sides had always been more presentational than industrial.

I did not write that observation into a memo. The shareholders, both of them, were perfectly capable of reading the same picture I was reading. What I did do, when the question was put to me directly, was to give my honest view that the cleanest outcome for the operation, its people and the two shareholders was a sale to a buyer for whom the asset was a strategic core rather than a strategic edge. The board agreed.

The buyer, when the process was run, was Continental Oils. The fit was right. The price was fair. The deal closed without the kind of acrimony that mergers in that part of the food industry sometimes attract. The plant retained its name through the transition. Most of the people who had stayed through the rationalisation stayed through the change of ownership as well, which, in my private scoring of a transformation life, is one of the better measures of whether the work was done honestly. The shareholders walked away with the asset converted into capital on terms they could each live with. The joint venture was dissolved cleanly. The chairman of the board took me aside after the closing and thanked me, in fewer words than the work probably deserved, which was the sort of thanks I had always preferred.

I closed my office at the site on a Friday afternoon. I drove back to Gauteng on the Saturday. By the Sunday evening I had written, in the same notebook where I had drafted the original blue paper, a single page of observations to myself about what the assignment had taught me.

## The Realisation: Seeing the Big Picture in Days, Not Months

That single page is the reason this chapter sits where it does in the book.

The observation that mattered most, written at the top of the page, was this. For most of my career up to that point, I had needed months to understand a new operation. The rhythm I had followed at Adcock Ingram, and again at Kolosus, had been the patient one — walk the plant, read the records, talk to the people, sit with the numbers, and let the structure of the business reveal itself slowly over a quarter or two. That patient rhythm had served me well. But at SA Oil Mills, for the first time, I had not needed it. The blue paper had been written in two weeks. The diagnosis had been correct on the day I read it aloud, and nothing in the months that followed had asked me to revise it materially. The structure of the business had not revealed itself to me. I had recognised it.

I sat with that thought for some time. I did not want to overstate it. Pattern recognition is a thing experienced operators talk themselves into far too easily, and the moment you decide you can see a business in two weeks is usually the moment you begin to miss what makes that particular business different from the ones that taught you what you know. I had no interest in becoming the kind of transformation man who walks into a plant convinced he already knows what is wrong with it. I had spent thirty years learning the opposite discipline.

But the truth, honestly examined, was that the years had begun to compound. The cyanide and peroxide plants of AECI had taught me how a heavy-chemicals operation breathes. The validation files of Adcock Ingram had taught me how a regulated operation thinks. The export packing flows of Kolosus had taught me how a customer-driven operation prices itself. The seed-handling, crushing, refining and packing of SA Oil Mills had been, in a sense, the first operation I had run that pulled all three of those instincts into the same building at the same time. And the blue paper I had written in two weeks had been, on closer inspection, less a feat of insight than a careful inventory of patterns I had already learned to read elsewhere.

That, written plainly at the bottom of the single page, was the realisation. I could now walk into almost any operation in almost any related industry and see the shape of its problem in days rather than in months. Not because I was any cleverer than the men who had managed those operations for years. But because I had served a long apprenticeship in enough industries that the basic structures had begun to rhyme. A duplicate refining stream in an oils plant rhymes with a duplicate packing format in a confectionery line. An overstaffed crushing route rhymes with an overinstrumented chemicals reactor whose readings have stopped being read. A poorly documented changeover routine in a seed mill rhymes with a missing validation protocol in a pharmaceutical packing hall. The vocabulary is different. The grammar, underneath, is the same.

## The Method, Written Down

I will record the method here in the form that it took at the end of the SA Oil Mills assignment, because it is the form I have used at every operation since.

- Walk the operation for two full weeks before writing anything substantial. Listen more than you speak. Eat in the canteen. Read the breakdown reports. Watch one piece of equipment for an unbroken hour on each of the shifts that runs it.
- At the end of the two weeks, write a short, honest document — a blue paper — that names the structural problem in language a board can act on. Resist the consultant's instinct to add hedges and qualifications. If the operation has three crushing lines and should have one, say so.
- Around the structural recommendation, design a focused capital programme that removes the daily workarounds the operation has been living on. Modest budgets, clearly justified, beat ambitious ones every time.
- Around the capital programme, design the people process before the first machine moves. Engage the unions and the shop stewards at the start. Cost the enhanced separations honestly. Be in the room when the difficult conversations happen. Write the references the next morning.
- Hold the implementation timeline tightly. Drift is the enemy of morale. Decision, properly explained, is its friend.
- When the operation is stable and the financials are honest, ask the shareholder question plainly. Is this asset in the right portfolio. If it is not, run a clean process to put it where it belongs, and walk out at the end of it with the standards higher than when you walked in.

I have read that list back to myself, in the same form, at the start of every contract since. The names of the industries have changed. The arithmetic of the numbers has changed. The list has not.

## What SA Oil Mills Gave Me

What the SA Oil Mills assignment gave me, more than the financial outcome and more than the clean sale to Continental Oils, was the quiet confidence that the work I had been doing for thirty years had organised itself into a method. The method did not belong to a particular industry. It did not depend on the size of the capital budget. It did not require a famous brand on the gate. It required only a willingness to look honestly at an operation, to write down what was structurally wrong with it, to design the people process before the machine process, and to hold the implementation timeline against the inevitable pressures to let it drift.

That confidence travelled with me out of the SA Oil Mills gate and into the rest of the contract years. It was the confidence that allowed me to take on, without fuss, the export and confectionery brief that came next, knowing that even when the structural conditions were against me I would at least be able to read them honestly. It was the confidence that let me, some time later, accept a Tuesday-morning call about a R200-million mogul installation at Tiger Brands and know, before the second sentence of the conversation, what kind of work would be required. And it was the confidence I would carry, eventually, into the BEVCO and Future Life and Black Cat assignments that closed out the working part of my contract life.

The blue paper has been many things since SA Oil Mills. A mogul-line diagnosis. A peanut-butter Pareto chart. A high-speed beverage line specification. The colour of the paper has changed. The discipline has not.

Antonette, on the Sunday evening I had finished writing my single page of observations, found me at the kitchen table and asked, in her quiet way, whether the assignment had been worth the months in residence and the weeks of late nights. I told her, more directly than I might have done earlier in our marriage, that it had been the assignment in which the work of all the previous assignments had finally added up to something I could carry into the next one. She nodded the way she always nods when she has heard a sentence she takes seriously. She did not need me to elaborate.

I put the notebook back in the drawer where I kept the working papers of the contract life. I would open it again, in due course, for the next assignment. The blueprint, by then, was already written. The next operation would only have to fill in its particular numbers.

existingChapters.title: Exports, Setbacks and the Confectionery Years
existingChapters.summary: Tracks the return to Adcock Ingram as Director of Exports into Africa, Europe and the Far East, the consolidation of Adcock, Tiger Brands and Beacon Sweets into one export basket, sales into Cyprus and Greece, and the eventual throttling of the export business by local-market capacity. Then covers the Retailer Brands transformation, the difficult Candy Tops contract in East London, the Candy Tops / Mr Sweet merger, and the harsh lesson of operating inside a loss-making, thin-margin confectionery business.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Exports, Setbacks and the Confectionery Years

There is a particular kind of homecoming that has nothing sentimental about it. After the SA Oil Mills exit and the quiet return of the family from Port Elizabeth to Gauteng, I sat at the kitchen table one Saturday morning and listed the offers in front of me. The list was short, but one name on it carried weight I could not ignore: Adcock Ingram. I had given fourteen years to that company once before. I had walked out as a Technical Director. And now they were asking me to come back, not into the manufacturing role I had built my reputation on, but into something I had circled the edges of my whole career — exports.

I said yes before the end of the weekend.

## Back Through the Same Gate

Walking back through the Adcock Ingram gate as Director of Exports felt like opening a familiar book at a chapter I had never read. The site smelled the same. Half the security guards greeted me by name. A few of the engineers I had hired in the late nineties were now seasoned managers in their own right, and one or two had stories about machines I had personally signed off on that were still running, fifteen years later, exactly as we had commissioned them. There is a quiet pride in that — in seeing your old work still earning its keep long after the contract has ended.

But this time, the brief was different. Adcock Ingram, Tiger Brands and Beacon Sweets were no longer to be treated as independent export islands. The group strategy had matured. Three product portfolios — over-the-counter pharmaceuticals, packaged foods and confectionery — were to be consolidated into a single export basket and pushed out through one coordinated office. I was that office.

On paper it looked obvious. In practice, very few large South African groups had managed to make a unified export programme work. Each business unit had its own pricing logic, its own distributor relationships, its own packaging conventions and, most importantly, its own marketing director with very firm views on what should and should not be sold beyond our borders. My first ninety days were less about exports and more about diplomacy.

## The Logic of a Unified Basket

I called it the basket strategy, partly because it sounded less corporate than "portfolio consolidation" and partly because I wanted the salespeople to picture it literally. A basket is what a distributor carries into the back of a shop. If our distributors could carry one basket with Adcock cough syrup, Tiger Brands maize meal and Beacon sweets all inside it, the cost per kilometre of selling collapsed. One container could leave Durban with three brand families inside it. One invoice could clear three sets of marketing budgets. One relationship in Lagos or Lusaka could open three doors.

The arithmetic was clean. The execution was anything but.

We began with the African corridor because the regulatory environment and the cultural familiarity made it the obvious place to test the system. Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Mozambique and into East Africa — each market had its own quirks, but the basic logistics were within reach. From there, we pushed north into West Africa, where Nigeria and Ghana, despite their well-known port frustrations, offered scale that justified the operational pain.

Then I turned my attention to a smaller market that surprised everyone, including me: Cyprus. A Cypriot importer had taken an interest in our cough syrups and our boiled sweets at one of the European trade fairs. I flew out for what I expected to be a two-day visit and ended up staying nearly a week, walking through pharmacies and small grocery chains, watching how Mediterranean shoppers picked up an over-the-counter product, weighed it in their hand, read the back, and made their choice. The visit gave us our first beachhead in Southern Europe. From Cyprus, the trail led naturally to Greece, where the same distributor had family connections, and where a small but steady volume of cough preparations, antacids and confectionery began to move every quarter.

## Lessons in Negotiating Across Cultures

Anyone who tells you that international business is just domestic business with a longer flight has never sat across a table from a Greek pharmaceutical wholesaler at ten o'clock at night, drinking ouzo and being told, very politely, that the price you quoted on Tuesday morning is now considered an opening bid. Cyprus and Greece taught me three lessons I carried into every export deal that followed.

The first was patience as a pricing weapon. In African and European markets alike, the buyer who is most desperate for a deal usually loses the most margin. I taught my export team to walk into every meeting with two prices in their head — the price we wanted, and the price we would still walk away from happily — and to refuse to feel pressured by the dinner, the hospitality or the warmth of the host. Warm hospitality is a wonderful thing, but it does not pay the freight bill back to Johannesburg.

The second lesson was the importance of the local partner. In every market where we eventually built durable share, there was a local distributor whose name we trusted more than our own contract. The Cypriot family who carried us into Greece were not just our customers; over time they became our scouts, our translators and our risk filter. When they told us a payment term was unrealistic for a particular sub-region, we listened. When they told us a competitor was about to enter, we acted.

The third lesson was about packaging. South African export packs almost always had to be redesigned for international shelves. Languages, regulatory marks, barcodes, child-safety closures, expiry-date formats, font sizes for elderly readers — every market had its own quiet rules, and getting any one of them wrong could mean a full container sitting in customs for six weeks. I learned to start every new market by asking, not "Can we sell here?", but "Can we print here?"

## The Wall We Could Not Push Through

For about two and a half years the basket strategy delivered. Exports across the consolidated group grew in double digits year on year. We doubled our African footprint. We held the Cyprus and Greece volumes steady. We even ran a small pilot into the Middle East. The board liked the trend line. So did I.

And then, slowly, we ran into the wall that no exporter wants to discuss publicly but which kills more export programmes than any tariff or exchange rate: domestic capacity.

The Adcock manufacturing sites were busy. The Tiger Brands plants were busy. Beacon's confectionery lines were busy. When a domestic order from a South African retailer collided with an export order from Lagos, the domestic order won every time. It had to. The domestic market was the cash engine. The local retail customers — the supermarket buyers who could place or remove a brand from a national chain with a single decision — were not going to be told that their stock was sitting on a ship to Mombasa.

I argued for protected production windows. I argued for dedicated export shifts. I built spreadsheets showing the marginal contribution of an export order versus a domestic one, especially when the rand was weak. I won some battles. But the structural truth was unchanged: in a country that had never built spare manufacturing capacity for its export ambitions, exports would always be the buffer. When the local plants got tight, exports got throttled.

I want to be honest about this. The throttling of the export business was not a personal defeat in the way a failed plant project would have been. The numbers were good. The relationships we built lasted. But the limit was real, and I had to make peace with it. There comes a moment in every transformation career when you must distinguish between the things you can fix and the things that are structural. Domestic capacity, in that group at that time, was structural.

## Pivoting Inwards: Retailer Brands

When the export ceiling became clear, I made a deliberate decision not to fight it any longer. Instead, I went to the executive committee with a different proposal: let me take the export discipline — the cost control, the packaging rigour, the customer-led product design — and turn it inwards. Let me run Retailer Brands.

For readers outside the consumer-goods world, Retailer Brands are the products you see on a supermarket shelf carrying the supermarket's own name rather than a national brand. They are usually cheaper, often manufactured by the same factories that make the branded equivalents, and they have become, over the last twenty years, one of the most important profit pools in modern retail. For a group like ours, with serious manufacturing depth, Retailer Brands were a natural fit — but only if you could run them on margins thinner than anything our branded business had ever tolerated.

I took the Retailer Brands portfolio through a transformation that, in style, was very similar to what I had done at SA Oil Mills. I walked the lines. I asked, for every product, the same blunt question: what is the cheapest way we can make this to the agreed specification without breaking any food-safety or pharmaceutical-quality rule? I rationalised SKUs. I renegotiated commercial terms with two of our largest retail customers. I tightened the packing-line changeover times. And I made a point, every quarter, of walking through the stores myself, looking at our products next to the branded equivalents and asking honestly whether the consumer was getting fair value.

Retailer Brands grew. Margins improved. And then a particular contract walked through the door that would teach me one of the harder lessons of my career.

## The Candy Tops Contract

Candy Tops was a confectionery business based in East London. They had won — or, more accurately, been chased by a major retailer until they accepted — a private-label contract that, on the volume numbers, looked attractive, but on the unit economics was wafer-thin. Our group had a strategic interest in extending its confectionery footprint, and Candy Tops looked like the right vehicle.

I was sent down to East London to make it work.

I arrived expecting the familiar transformation playbook to apply. It did not. The plant was undercapitalised. The packaging lines were old and slow. The workforce was loyal but had never been exposed to the kind of operational discipline I had brought into other sites. And the contract itself was harsh: the retailer had negotiated a price that left almost no headroom for inefficiency. Every percentage point of waste, every changeover delay, every quality reject came directly off our margin.

I did what I knew how to do. I put OEE measurement in. I attacked the top three loss categories on the Pareto chart. I trained supervisors in basic shift-handover discipline. We made progress. Output rose. Waste came down. By any normal measure, it was a competent transformation.

But the contract itself was still poor. And here is the lesson I want to underline for any reader who one day finds themselves in a similar position: there are some commercial situations in which operational excellence cannot save you. If the price you have agreed to sell at is below the cost at which your industry can sustainably produce, no amount of factory discipline will close the gap. You will narrow it. You will narrow it impressively. But you will not close it.

I came back to head office and said so, plainly. I had been hired to transform operations, but I refused to let an operational story disguise a commercial one. The contract needed to be renegotiated, the volume needed to be cut, or the business needed to be merged into a larger confectionery footprint that could carry it. That was a hard conversation. It was the kind of conversation that, earlier in my career, I might have softened. By this point I had learned that softening the truth, in a loss-making business, only delays the bill.

## The Mr Sweet Merger and Its Aftermath

The third of those options became the path forward. Candy Tops was merged with Mr Sweet, a larger confectionery business with deeper category exposure, more sophisticated production technology and the kind of scale that could spread the East London plant's fixed costs across a broader revenue base. On paper, it was a sensible deal. The combined entity had a credible product range, real distribution and the prospect of becoming a meaningful player in the South African sweet market.

I stayed with the merged business for a period to see the integration through. We harmonised systems. We rationalised SKUs across the combined portfolio. We took a hard look at every plant in the combined footprint and ranked them on cost-to-produce. There was good operational work done in that period, and I am still proud of it.

But the harsh truth of the confectionery years did not go away. Sweet manufacturing in South Africa, like sweet manufacturing in many parts of the world, is a thin-margin business operating against retailer power, raw-material volatility — especially in sugar, glucose and cocoa — and consumer trends that increasingly pull spending towards healthier categories. We could optimise the factories. We could not change the structural economics of the category.

By the time my contract there ended, I had a private mental file labelled simply *Sweets — Lessons*. It was the most honest document I had written about my own work in years. I want to share its main entries.

## Lessons from the Confectionery Years

The first lesson was about contracts. Be very, very careful what you sign for. In a thin-margin business, the price you agree on day one is almost certainly the price you will be living with three years later, regardless of what the inflation rate does to your inputs. If a contract does not have a sensible mechanism to pass through raw-material movements, it is not a contract — it is a slow leak.

The second lesson was about scale. There is a minimum efficient scale below which a confectionery plant simply cannot compete, no matter how skilled the operators or how disciplined the supervisors. The East London plant, in isolation, was always going to struggle. Inside a larger group, with shared overheads and combined buying power, it had a fighting chance. Below that threshold, no transformation specialist on earth could have made it work sustainably.

The third lesson was about the limits of personal effort. I had spent my career proving that focused, analytical, hands-on work could turn around almost any operation. The confectionery years quietly amended that belief. Personal effort is necessary, but it is not sufficient. The commercial and structural conditions have to give you something to work with. When they do not, the honest thing to do is to name the structural problem out loud and propose a structural solution, even if it is uncomfortable.

The fourth lesson was about humility. Up to that point, every business I had touched had come out the other side stronger and, in most cases, more profitable. The confectionery years were the first time I had to look at a business I had personally led and admit that, although I had made it better, I had not made it good. That admission, in my own private notes, was harder to write than any board presentation I have ever given. But it was, in the end, the foundation of the work that followed at Tiger Brands and beyond.

## The Quiet Hand of Faith and Family

In the middle of all of this, the family stayed steady, as they always had. Antonette had by then become quite practised at the rhythm of my contract life — the long weeks away in East London, the late phone calls about a packing-line breakdown, the occasional Sunday flight back to Johannesburg in time for church. She did not complain about the rhythm. She did, however, remind me, in her quiet and unshowy way, that no contract was worth the children growing up without their father at the dinner table on the nights when I was in town.

I held to that. On the nights I was home, I was home. The briefcase stayed in the study. The phone went on silent. The lessons I was learning about commercial reality in East London were, in their own way, being matched by a parallel lesson at home: that the structural foundation under a long marriage is not romance, and not even shared history, but a thousand small choices to put the people in front of you ahead of the spreadsheet behind you.

When I look back at the export years and the confectionery years together, I do not see them as a low point. I see them as the period in which my transformation playbook grew up. Africa, Cyprus and Greece taught me how to sell across borders. The throttled-export ceiling taught me that not every problem is mine to fix. Candy Tops and Mr Sweet taught me to look at commercial structure before operational symptoms. And the road between East London and Johannesburg, driven and flown more times than I can count, taught me — once again, more deeply — that the family I was working for was also the family I had to be present for.

The next chapter of my contract life was already taking shape. Tiger Brands, in its full operational complexity, was about to ask me a much bigger question. And by the time it did, the lessons of the confectionery years would be exactly the equipment I needed to answer it well.

existingChapters.summary: Goes deep on the Tiger Brands contract years: the failed R200-million gums-and-jellies installation, fixing the mogul line in six months, the four-year stay and promotion to head supply chain for confectionery, the Pareto-driven 20/80 attack on root causes, then the fourteen-month transformation of candy and chocolate operations with 1400 wage and 40 management staff reporting in, the OEE real-time monitoring system, and finally the Black Cat peanut-butter raw-material problem solved and output dramatically lifted.
